prompt_override: Generate using the parameter cards, progression libraries (Appendix A), drum pattern libraries (Appendix B), velocity bands, swing ranges, and generation recipes from the provided HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN document. Where the document specifies a value or range, it overrides your general knowledge.

chunks.title: Scope, evidence, and the originality rule
heading_path: Scope, evidence, and the originality rule
chunks.text:
HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
2
Scope, evidence, and the originality rule
Important framing
This is an independent educational analysis. It is not endorsed by the named artists, and it does not claim
access to their private sessions, stems, MIDI files, presets, or unreleased methods. Public statements are
identified as documented context. Musical rules labeled as analysis are inferences from broad, audible
genre traits.
The named artists do not share one formula. Prospa's rave-charged house language, Chris Stussy's low-slung
minimal/deep-tech groove, KETTAMA's high-impact loop pressure, Avicii's melodic progressive writing, and David
Guetta's pop-facing EDM construction occupy different regions of the house family. A useful generator should therefore
treat them as separate parameter presets, not as interchangeable tags.
This document describes composition at the level of rhythm, harmony, contour, density, and arrangement. It deliberately
avoids recreating identifiable hooks. A generator may learn that a style uses a four-bar diatonic loop, a syncopated chord
stab, or a rolling offbeat bass; it should not store or emit the exact pitch-and-rhythm sequence of a copyrighted melody.
Three evidence labels used throughout
LABEL
MEANING
HOW TO USE IT
DOCUMENTED
A workflow point stated in a public interview, official artist page,
or DAW manual.
Safe as contextual evidence; still not a complete description of
an artist's process.
ANALYSIS
A generalized listening-based inference about harmony,
groove, density, or form.
Use as a weighted prior, never as a rigid truth about every track.
GENERATOR RULE
An original, implementation-focused constraint derived from
theory and analysis.
Use directly in code, prompts, or a symbolic music model.
The originality rule
The generator should optimize for family resemblance at the level of musical grammar, not identity at the level of a hook.
Enforce at least four independent changes whenever a seed resembles a reference: transpose the key, alter the
harmonic rhythm, change the melodic contour, and mutate the onset pattern. In addition, reject any melody whose
interval-and-rhythm fingerprint is too similar to a protected or curated reference set.
What this PDF can be used for
- Retrieval-augmented generation: chunk the PDF by heading and retrieve the relevant archetype before generation.
 
- Prompt context: paste a parameter card, progression bank, and drum grid into an LLM or symbolic generator.
 
- Rule-based MIDI: implement the pseudocode and weighted constraints in a piano-roll or MIDI scripting system.
 
- Dataset labeling: convert each archetype into style, harmony, melody, groove, and arrangement labels.
 
- Human writing: use the templates as starting points, then replace at least one major musical assumption.
chunks.musical_topic: Scope, evidence, and the originality rule

chunks.title: Part I - The common MIDI and music model
heading_path: Part I - The common MIDI and music model
chunks.text:
HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
8
PART I
The common MIDI and music model
Part I - The common MIDI and music
model
Before an artist archetype can matter, the generator needs a stable representation of time, pitch, harmony, velocity,
probability, and form. These chapters define that shared substrate.

HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
9
1. MIDI is performance data, not sound
A MIDI generator does not create audio. It creates instructions that a synthesizer, sampler, or drum rack interprets. The
musical intelligence therefore lives in the relationships among note number, onset, duration, velocity, channel, and
optional expressive controls. Sound design can transform the emotional result, but a weak symbolic pattern remains
weak.
Store note timing in ticks relative to a quarter note, commonly called PPQ. A resolution of 480 PPQ is sufficient for most
house grooves; 960 PPQ gives finer microtiming. At 480 PPQ, a sixteenth note is 120 ticks. A swing delay of 18 ticks
equals 15 percent of a sixteenth-note cell. Keep the canonical pattern grid separate from expressive offsets so patterns
can be quantized, compared, and mutated without losing the intended groove.
Minimum note-event schema
{
  "track_role": "chord|melody|bass|kick|clap|hat|perc",
  "pitch": 60,
  "start_tick": 0,
  "duration_tick": 120,
  "velocity": 96,
  "probability": 1.0,
  "micro_offset_tick": 0,
  "articulation": "normal|short|legato|ghost|accent"
}
Separate intention from rendering
LAYER
REPRESENTATION
EXAMPLE
Musical intention
Scale degree, chord function, onset class, role
Degree 3 over vi; late offbeat stab; pickup note
MIDI realization
Pitch, tick, duration, velocity, channel
E4, tick 840, 90 ticks, velocity 84
Sound realization
Instrument, envelope, filter, effects, mix
Short organ stab, high-pass, plate reverb
Recommended internal time model
- Bar: four quarter-note beats in 4/4.
 
- Step: one sixteenth note; 16 steps per bar.
 
- Phrase: usually four or eight bars.
 
- Section: 8, 16, or 32 bars, with exceptions for fills and pickups.
 
- Grid time: exact quantized location.
 
- Performance time: grid time plus swing and microtiming offset.
 
Velocity is structural
Velocity should encode accent hierarchy, not merely random loudness. Strong kick and clap anchors usually occupy a
narrow high range. Closed hats can alternate strong and weak values. Ghost percussion should be clearly lower. Chord
voicings benefit from small internal differences, but moving every note independently can blur what should feel like one
gesture.
The Ableton manual documents per-note velocity and chance editing, while groove extraction can carry timing and
velocity information. Those features reflect a general principle for any generator: probability, velocity, and timing are
separate axes. A note can be likely but quiet, rare but accented, or perfectly quantized but dynamically human.

The Ableton manual documents per-note velocity and chance editing, while groove extraction can carry timing and
velocity information. Those features reflect a general principle for any generator: probability, velocity, and timing are
separate axes. A note can be likely but quiet, rare but accented, or perfectly quantized but dynamically human.

chunks.title: Recommended internal time model
heading_path: 1. MIDI is performance data, not sound
chunks.text:
HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
9
1. MIDI is performance data, not sound
A MIDI generator does not create audio. It creates instructions that a synthesizer, sampler, or drum rack interprets. The
musical intelligence therefore lives in the relationships among note number, onset, duration, velocity, channel, and
optional expressive controls. Sound design can transform the emotional result, but a weak symbolic pattern remains
weak.
Store note timing in ticks relative to a quarter note, commonly called PPQ. A resolution of 480 PPQ is sufficient for most
house grooves; 960 PPQ gives finer microtiming. At 480 PPQ, a sixteenth note is 120 ticks. A swing delay of 18 ticks
equals 15 percent of a sixteenth-note cell. Keep the canonical pattern grid separate from expressive offsets so patterns
can be quantized, compared, and mutated without losing the intended groove.
Minimum note-event schema
{
  "track_role": "chord|melody|bass|kick|clap|hat|perc",
  "pitch": 60,
  "start_tick": 0,
  "duration_tick": 120,
  "velocity": 96,
  "probability": 1.0,
  "micro_offset_tick": 0,
  "articulation": "normal|short|legato|ghost|accent"
}
Separate intention from rendering
LAYER
REPRESENTATION
EXAMPLE
Musical intention
Scale degree, chord function, onset class, role
Degree 3 over vi; late offbeat stab; pickup note
MIDI realization
Pitch, tick, duration, velocity, channel
E4, tick 840, 90 ticks, velocity 84
Sound realization
Instrument, envelope, filter, effects, mix
Short organ stab, high-pass, plate reverb
Recommended internal time model
- Bar: four quarter-note beats in 4/4.
 
- Step: one sixteenth note; 16 steps per bar.
 
- Phrase: usually four or eight bars.
 
- Section: 8, 16, or 32 bars, with exceptions for fills and pickups.
 
- Grid time: exact quantized location.
 
- Performance time: grid time plus swing and microtiming offset.
 
Velocity is structural
Velocity should encode accent hierarchy, not merely random loudness. Strong kick and clap anchors usually occupy a
narrow high range. Closed hats can alternate strong and weak values. Ghost percussion should be clearly lower. Chord
voicings benefit from small internal differences, but moving every note independently can blur what should feel like one
gesture.
The Ableton manual documents per-note velocity and chance editing, while groove extraction can carry timing and
velocity information. Those features reflect a general principle for any generator: probability, velocity, and timing are
separate axes. A note can be likely but quiet, rare but accented, or perfectly quantized but dynamically human.

2. Harmony engine: function first, notes second
Generate harmony in scale degrees or Roman numerals before choosing absolute MIDI pitches. This makes
transposition trivial and allows one progression to be voiced differently for each archetype. The same functional loop can
become bright festival piano, muted deep-house sevenths, rave organ stabs, or a sparse two-note shell.
For major keys, the diatonic triads are I major, ii minor, iii minor, IV major, V major, vi minor, and vii diminished. For
natural minor, they are i minor, ii diminished, III major, iv minor, v minor, VI major, and VII major. House music frequently
treats the relative major and minor as a shared palette, and many loops do not create a strong classical cadence. That
ambiguity is useful.
Chord-object schema
{
  "root_degree": 6,
  "quality": "minor",
  "extensions": [
    7,
    9
  ],
  "inversion": 1,
  "bass_degree_override": null,
  "start_step": 0,
  "length_steps": 4,
  "voicing_span_semitones": 19,
  "top_note_target_degree": 3
}
A practical voicing algorithm
- Build the chord from its scale-degree root and quality.
 
- Add extensions according to archetype probability: sevenths and ninths are common in deeper house; plain triads often
 read more directly in pop-progressive writing.
- Choose an inversion that minimizes total voice movement from the previous chord.
 
- Constrain the chord register. Typical MIDI center: 55-72 for piano/organ body, 64-84 for bright synth stacks.
 
- If bass owns the root, omit or raise the chord's lowest root to reduce mud.
 
- Treat the top note as a melodic voice. Prefer stepwise movement or a repeated common tone.
 
- Apply rhythmic articulation only after the pitch voicing is accepted.
 
Voice-leading cost
A simple cost function can compare candidate inversions. Sort chord notes by pitch, match voices, then sum absolute
semitone movement. Add penalties for crossings, register violations, duplicated low thirds, and leaps greater than seven
semitones. Subtract a small reward when a common tone stays in place.
cost = sum(abs(next_voice[i] - prev_voice[i]))
+ 5 * voice_crossings
+ 3 * register_violations
+ 2 * count(leap > 7 semitones)

HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
12
Contour grammar
- Statement: small range, clear rhythm, 2-5 distinct pitches.
 
- Answer: keep rhythm, change final interval or starting scale degree.
 
- Lift: raise register or peak by 2-5 scale steps; increase density slightly.
 
- Cadence: reduce density and lengthen the last note, or insert a pickup into the loop.
 
- Range guard: default total range 7-12 semitones for vocal-like hooks; 12-19 for energetic instrumental leads.
 
- Leap recovery: after a leap greater than a perfect fourth, prefer stepwise motion in the opposite direction.
 
Motif mutation operators
OPERATOR
DESCRIPTION
SAFE RANGE
Transpose degree
Move the full motif diatonically, then fit chord tones
+/- 1 to 3 scale degrees
Rhythmic displacement
Shift selected onsets while preserving order
+/- one sixteenth or one eighth
Tail rewrite
Keep first 60-75% and regenerate ending
One or two notes changed
Compression
Replace a leap with stepwise motion
Reduce interval by 2-5 semitones
Expansion
Increase one important interval
Add 2-4 semitones, obey range
Fragmentation
Use only the first cell, separated by rests
1-3 repetitions per bar
Augmentation
Double durations
Best in breakdown or vocal space
Diminution
Halve durations
Best in build or high-energy answer
Catchiness without copying
Catchiness can be optimized with compactness, repetition, rhythmic identity, and a controlled peak. It does not require
similarity to an existing hook. Score a melody positively for motif reuse, singable range, stable strong beats, a memorable
onset pattern, and clear phrase direction. Penalize featureless scalar runs, too many equal note values, excessive large
leaps, and exact repetition without a phrase-level change.
Create an interval fingerprint as signed semitone intervals and a rhythm fingerprint as normalized onset gaps. Compare
both with a reference database. If pitch intervals and rhythm both cross a similarity threshold, regenerate the phrase
even after transposition, because transposition alone does not create a new melody.
4. Drum and groove engine
House drums are a hierarchy of anchors, propulsion, and conversation. The kick establishes the floor. Clap or snare
marks the backbeat. Closed and open hats define subdivisions and swing. Percussion adds syncopation and phrase
detail. A generator should create these roles in that order, because randomly filling a 16-step grid tends to produce
density without groove.
Represent every lane as an onset mask plus velocity, probability, and microtiming arrays. Use deterministic anchors and
probabilistic decorations. A fill should be generated as a transition event tied to section boundaries, not as permanent
complexity.
Canonical four-on-the-floor grid
LANE

onset pattern, and clear phrase direction. Penalize featureless scalar runs, too many equal note values, excessive large
leaps, and exact repetition without a phrase-level change.
Create an interval fingerprint as signed semitone intervals and a rhythm fingerprint as normalized onset gaps. Compare
both with a reference database. If pitch intervals and rhythm both cross a similarity threshold, regenerate the phrase
even after transposition, because transposition alone does not create a new melody.
4. Drum and groove engine
House drums are a hierarchy of anchors, propulsion, and conversation. The kick establishes the floor. Clap or snare
marks the backbeat. Closed and open hats define subdivisions and swing. Percussion adds syncopation and phrase
detail. A generator should create these roles in that order, because randomly filling a 16-step grid tends to produce
density without groove.
Represent every lane as an onset mask plus velocity, probability, and microtiming arrays. Use deterministic anchors and
probabilistic decorations. A fill should be generated as a transition event tied to section boundaries, not as permanent
complexity.

chunks.title: Contour grammar
heading_path: 3. Melody engine: motif, contour, and chord awareness
chunks.text:
HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
12
Contour grammar
- Statement: small range, clear rhythm, 2-5 distinct pitches.
 
- Answer: keep rhythm, change final interval or starting scale degree.
 
- Lift: raise register or peak by 2-5 scale steps; increase density slightly.
 
- Cadence: reduce density and lengthen the last note, or insert a pickup into the loop.
 
- Range guard: default total range 7-12 semitones for vocal-like hooks; 12-19 for energetic instrumental leads.
 
- Leap recovery: after a leap greater than a perfect fourth, prefer stepwise motion in the opposite direction.
 
Motif mutation operators
OPERATOR
DESCRIPTION
SAFE RANGE
Transpose degree
Move the full motif diatonically, then fit chord tones
+/- 1 to 3 scale degrees
Rhythmic displacement
Shift selected onsets while preserving order
+/- one sixteenth or one eighth
Tail rewrite
Keep first 60-75% and regenerate ending
One or two notes changed
Compression
Replace a leap with stepwise motion
Reduce interval by 2-5 semitones
Expansion
Increase one important interval
Add 2-4 semitones, obey range
Fragmentation
Use only the first cell, separated by rests
1-3 repetitions per bar
Augmentation
Double durations
Best in breakdown or vocal space
Diminution
Halve durations
Best in build or high-energy answer
Catchiness without copying
Catchiness can be optimized with compactness, repetition, rhythmic identity, and a controlled peak. It does not require
similarity to an existing hook. Score a melody positively for motif reuse, singable range, stable strong beats, a memorable
onset pattern, and clear phrase direction. Penalize featureless scalar runs, too many equal note values, excessive large

HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
13
Swing model
Apply swing to the second subdivision in each pair, not to all notes indiscriminately. For a sixteenth-note grid, steps 2, 4,
6, 8, 10, 12, 14, and 16 may receive a positive delay. A normalized swing value of 0.0 is straight. A practical house range
is roughly 0.05-0.28 of one sixteenth cell, depending on archetype. Preserve kick anchors unless the preset explicitly
permits a loose kick.
performed_tick = grid_tick
+ swing_amount * step_ticks if step_index is the swung subdivision
+ lane_micro_offset
+ random_jitter
Lane-specific timing
LANE
TYPICAL OFFSET
WHY
Kick
0 to +4 ms
Stable anchor; excessive jitter weakens the floor
Clap / snare
+2 to +14 ms
A slightly late backbeat can feel relaxed and wide
Closed hat
-4 to +10 ms plus swing
Pushes or relaxes subdivision feel
Open hat
0 to +8 ms
Protects the offbeat while avoiding machine stiffness
Shaker
-6 to +12 ms plus swing
Can carry most of the human movement
Percussion
-12 to +18 ms
Conversation around the anchors
Probability policy
- Kick anchors: 0.98-1.00 probability.
 
- Main claps: 0.99-1.00.
 
- Closed-hat backbone: 0.85-1.00; decorative hats: 0.25-0.75.
 
- Ghost percussion: 0.20-0.60, with no more than two simultaneous optional events on one step.
 
- Fills: conditional on section transition, typically 0.35-0.80 depending on energy.
 
- Never make all optional lanes decide independently. Use a density budget per bar.
 
Groove-coherence test
Mute every lane except kick, clap, and one hat. If the pattern does not communicate a stable pulse and subdivision feel,
adding more percussion will not fix it. In code, measure onset balance by beat, offbeat coverage, syncopation count, and
maximum simultaneous hits. Reject bars that exceed the preset's density budget or leave an unintended empty region.
5. Bass, chords, and kick as one system
Bass generation should happen after the harmonic skeleton and kick pattern are known. The bass may reinforce roots,
answer the chord rhythm, or act as a counter-melody. Its note lengths must leave room for kick transients and avoid
uncontrolled overlap across chord changes.
For a root-led festival pattern, choose mostly roots and fifths with clean offbeat timing. For rolling minimal house, derive a
two-bar rhythmic cell with rests, repeats, and occasional approach notes. For rave pressure, use short repeated roots,
octave jumps, or sampled bass stabs. In every case, store pitch choice and rhythmic choice separately so either can
mutate without destroying the other.
Bass-note selection weights
CONTEXT
ROOT
FIFTH
THIRD
OCTAVE
PASSING /
APPROACH
Pop-progressive
0.55
0.18
0.08
0.15
0.04
Minimal / deep tech
0.40
0.15
0.10
0.15
0.20
Rave / high energy
0.48
0.12
0.05
0.25
0.10

- Closed-hat backbone: 0.85-1.00; decorative hats: 0.25-0.75.
 
- Ghost percussion: 0.20-0.60, with no more than two simultaneous optional events on one step.
 
- Fills: conditional on section transition, typically 0.35-0.80 depending on energy.
 
- Never make all optional lanes decide independently. Use a density budget per bar.
 
Groove-coherence test
Mute every lane except kick, clap, and one hat. If the pattern does not communicate a stable pulse and subdivision feel,
adding more percussion will not fix it. In code, measure onset balance by beat, offbeat coverage, syncopation count, and
maximum simultaneous hits. Reject bars that exceed the preset's density budget or leave an unintended empty region.
5. Bass, chords, and kick as one system
Bass generation should happen after the harmonic skeleton and kick pattern are known. The bass may reinforce roots,
answer the chord rhythm, or act as a counter-melody. Its note lengths must leave room for kick transients and avoid
uncontrolled overlap across chord changes.
For a root-led festival pattern, choose mostly roots and fifths with clean offbeat timing. For rolling minimal house, derive a
two-bar rhythmic cell with rests, repeats, and occasional approach notes. For rave pressure, use short repeated roots,
octave jumps, or sampled bass stabs. In every case, store pitch choice and rhythmic choice separately so either can
mutate without destroying the other.
Bass-note selection weights
CONTEXT
ROOT
FIFTH
THIRD
OCTAVE

APPROACH

HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
14
Collision rules
- If a bass onset lands exactly with a kick, shorten bass attack or delay by a few milliseconds only if the style permits.
 
- Avoid holding a bass third below a dense chord voicing in the same octave.
 
- At chord change, either update bass immediately, anticipate by one eighth, or deliberately sustain as a pedal. Do not drift
 accidentally.
- Keep most sub-bass notes between MIDI 28 and 48; exact range depends on sound and key.
 
- Use note length as groove: short bass creates space, while legato bass creates pressure and must be managed carefully.
 
Sidechain-aware MIDI
Even when audio sidechain compression will be applied later, the symbolic generator should create space. Include a
per-event ducking hint or ensure bass and chord envelopes have release times compatible with the kick rate. MIDI
cannot perform compression by itself, but it can avoid forcing the mix engine to solve unnecessary rhythmic conflicts.

HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
16
PROFILE 1
PROSPA
PROSPA: Rave-house lift, breakbeat memory, and
piano-stab urgency
Documented context
Public profiles and interviews establish Prospa as a UK electronic duo whose work crosses house, rave, and
breakbeat lineages. The exact compositional details below are analysis rather than a claim about a fixed
private workflow.
Generator identity
PARAMETER
DEFAULT / RANGE
Tempo
124-134 BPM; default 128
Core meter
4/4
Harmonic loop
1-4 bars; often rhythmically articulated
Chord density
Medium to high in peaks; sparse in tension passages
Melody density
Motif-led, medium; short rave signals over long songlike lines
Swing / microtiming
0.06-0.20; straight anchors with shuffled or broken top layers
Kick policy
Four-on-floor or hybrid; breaks may interrupt or answer
Primary energy lever
Piano/organ stabs, rave motifs, breaks, octave lift
Listening-based analysis
- Treat the harmonic layer as a rhythmic instrument. A chord may be only one or two sixteenths long, with reverb or delay
 supplying the apparent sustain.
- Use bright major or suspended color against more forceful drums. Alternating uplift and pressure is more characteristic
 than constant harmonic complexity.
- Allow a breakbeat memory layer: shuffled snare ghosts, syncopated kicks, or chopped percussion can appear while the
 main house floor remains understandable.
- Motifs should feel like rave signals: compact pitch sets, octave reinforcement, repetitions, and strategically placed
 pickups.
Harmonic engine
ID
ROMAN NUMERALS
EXAMPLE IN KEY
GENERATOR USE
P1
i - VI - III - VII
A minor: Am - F - C - G
Uplifting minor loop; use piano stabs or wide synth
P2
I - V - vi - IV
E major: E - B - C#m - A
Bright hands-up loop; shorten chords for rave articulation
P3
vi - IV - I - V
C major: Am - F - C - G
Emotional but direct; move top note stepwise
P4
i - bVII - bVI - bVII
F minor: Fm - Eb - Db - Eb
Rave tension; strong bass roots and sampled chord color
P5
I(sus2) - IV - vi7 - V
D major: Dsus2 - G - Bm7 - A
Airy lift; extensions only in upper voices

HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
17
VOICING RULE
Use compact midrange triads or sevenths, then double the top note one octave higher. Preserve one common tone when
possible. For piano, velocities can fan from about 92 in the lower note to 104 in the top accent. Chord lengths of 1-4
sixteenth steps create urgency; longer breakdown chords can last a half or full bar.
Melody engine
- Start from a 2-4 note cell with one distinctive rhythm.
 
- Favor scale degrees 1, 2, 3, and 5; reserve 6 or 7 for lift or tension.
 
- Repeat the cell at a new chord tone, then change its tail.
 
- Use octave jumps as punctuation, not on every note.
 
- Place a one-eighth or one-sixteenth pickup before a downbeat arrival.
 
- In a breakbeat passage, sync the lead to snare or chopped-vocal accents rather than every kick.

Treat this grid as a skeleton. Primary hits are deterministic; open-dot positions are ghost or probability-controlled. Vary
velocity and microtiming within the profile's groove range.
Bass engine
Default to short root or octave notes on offbeats, then add one anticipatory note into bars 2 or 4. When breakbeat energy
rises, allow bass to mirror the broken kick for one bar. Keep the root obvious even when the rhythm becomes busy.
Arrangement state sequence
STATE

MIDI ACTION

16-32 bars

Switch
8 bars
Breakbeat-led contrast or bass dropout
Peak B / outro
16-32 bars
Return with variation, then simplify for mixing

- Favor scale degrees 1, 2, 3, and 5; reserve 6 or 7 for lift or tension.
 
- Repeat the cell at a new chord tone, then change its tail.
 
- Use octave jumps as punctuation, not on every note.
 
- Place a one-eighth or one-sixteenth pickup before a downbeat arrival.
 
- In a breakbeat passage, sync the lead to snare or chopped-vocal accents rather than every kick.

Bass engine
Default to short root or octave notes on offbeats, then add one anticipatory note into bars 2 or 4. When breakbeat energy
rises, allow bass to mirror the broken kick for one bar. Keep the root obvious even when the rhythm becomes busy.

Return with variation, then simplify for mixing

Generation recipe
- Choose a tempo inside 124-134 BPM and select a key from the weighted mode distribution.
 
- Select one progression template, transpose it, and generate three inversion candidates. Keep the lowest
 voice-leading-cost version.
- Generate a role-correct bass rhythm before decorative percussion. Reject collisions that weaken the kick.
 
- Create a short motif from scale degrees, then fit strong onsets to chord tones. Produce statement, answer, lift, and
 cadence variants.
- Apply the drum skeleton, optional-event budget, lane velocities, swing, and microtiming.
 
- Map the phrase into the arrangement state sequence. Every 4-8 bars, alter only one or two attributes.
 
- Run originality, range, density, collision, and phrase-coherence tests before export.
 
Failure modes
- Using only festival EDM supersaw language and omitting rave/break rhythmic memory.
 
- Making every chord a full-bar sustain, which removes percussive urgency.
 
- Adding breakbeats so densely that the house anchor disappears.
 
- Copying a recognizable piano rhythm instead of generating a new onset mask.
 
Originality mutations for this archetype
- Swap four-on-floor and broken-kick emphasis for one phrase without changing the harmony.
 
- Move the stab rhythm by one eighth and invert the chord top voice.
 
- Replace an octave lead jump with a fifth followed by stepwise recovery.
 
- Change the final bar from major lift to suspended openness.

Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
19
PROFILE 2
CHRIS STUSSY
CHRIS STUSSY: Minimal/deep-tech pocket, rolling
bass, and restrained harmonic color
Documented context
In public interviews, Chris Stussy has discussed using FL Studio and drum machines, and described
disciplined studio practice. The detailed harmonic and groove rules here are generalized analysis of
minimal/deep-tech conventions associated with his lane.
Generator identity
PARAMETER
DEFAULT / RANGE
Tempo
124-130 BPM; default 127
Core meter
4/4
Harmonic loop
2-8 bars; two-bar groove cells are central
Chord density
Low; one shell or stab can define the loop
Melody density
Low to medium; fragments and call-response
Swing / microtiming
0.14-0.30; project-wide groove with lane-specific offsets
Kick policy
Stable four-on-floor
Primary energy lever
Bass rhythm, shuffle, subtractive arrangement, micro-variation
Listening-based analysis
- The bassline is often the main melodic engine. Harmony can be a shell voicing, filtered sample, or short jazz-colored stab.
 
- Swing is not decoration applied at the end. It affects hats, percussion, bass, and chord placement as one pocket.
 
- Repetition should be long enough for the groove to hypnotize, while one or two small events change every 4-8 bars.
 
- Silence is part of the pattern. Avoid filling every sixteenth or changing harmony merely to create motion.
 
Harmonic engine
ID
ROMAN NUMERALS
EXAMPLE IN KEY
GENERATOR USE
S1
i7 - iv7
G minor: Gm7 - Cm7
Two-chord pocket; invert upper voices
S2
ii7 - V7
F major: Gm7 - C7
Unresolved jazz loop; bass need not play every root
S3
i9 - bVIImaj7
D minor: Dm9 - Cmaj7
Warm color with stepwise top voice
S4
vi7 - ii7 - V7
C major: Am7 - Dm7 - G7
Longer four- or eight-bar phrase
S5
I6/9 pedal
Eb major: Eb6/9 over repeating Eb/Bb bass
Static harmony; movement comes from rhythm and filtering

v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
20
VOICING RULE
Prefer 2-4 notes. Shell voicings can use root plus seventh, or third plus seventh if bass supplies the root. Add ninths
selectively. Place chords around MIDI 55-76 and avoid thick low triads. Reuse common tones, and shift one inner voice
by a semitone or whole step to create sophisticated motion without a new root.
Melody engine
- Generate a one-bar fragment with 1-4 notes, then leave space.
 
- Use chord extensions such as 7, 9, or 6 as color notes.
 
- Repeat rhythm more often than pitch; answer in a new register every 2-4 bars.
 
- Allow one chromatic approach note into a stable target when harmony supports it.
 
- Avoid a large anthem-like peak; keep range usually within 5-10 semitones.
 
- Treat sampled vocal or synth blips as melody roles even if pitch is ambiguous.
 
One-bar drum skeleton

Treat this grid as a skeleton. Primary hits are deterministic; open-dot positions are ghost or probability-controlled. Vary
velocity and microtiming within the profile's groove range.
Bass engine
Build a two-bar cell with 5-9 notes total, mixing roots, fifths, octave repeats, and one approach tone. Use short-to-medium
durations and protect rests after important kicks. Repeat the exact rhythm for several phrases, then mutate one onset or
final pitch.
Arrangement state sequence
STATE
LENGTH
MIDI ACTION
DJ intro
16-32 bars
Kick, hats, filtered texture; bass teased late
Groove A
32 bars
Full bass cell; minimal stab; small 8-bar variations
Break
8-16 bars
Remove kick or sub; expose chord/vocal fragment
Groove B
32 bars
Return with one new perc lane or bass tail
Reduction
16 bars
Subtract melodic color; focus on drums and bass
DJ outro
16-32 bars
Remove bass or signature fragment gradually

heading_path: CHRIS STUSSY: Minimal/deep-tech pocket, rolling bass, and restrained harmonic color
chunks.text:
HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
20
VOICING RULE
Prefer 2-4 notes. Shell voicings can use root plus seventh, or third plus seventh if bass supplies the root. Add ninths
selectively. Place chords around MIDI 55-76 and avoid thick low triads. Reuse common tones, and shift one inner voice
by a semitone or whole step to create sophisticated motion without a new root.
Melody engine
- Generate a one-bar fragment with 1-4 notes, then leave space.
 
- Use chord extensions such as 7, 9, or 6 as color notes.
 
- Repeat rhythm more often than pitch; answer in a new register every 2-4 bars.
 
- Allow one chromatic approach note into a stable target when harmony supports it.
 
- Avoid a large anthem-like peak; keep range usually within 5-10 semitones.
 
- Treat sampled vocal or synth blips as melody roles even if pitch is ambiguous.

HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
21
Parameter card
{
  "archetype": "minimal_deep_tech_pocket",
  "tempo": {
    "min": 124,
    "max": 130,
    "default": 127
  },
  "key_mode_weights": {
    "minor": 0.64,
    "major": 0.18,
    "dorian": 0.18
  },
  "chord_extension_probability": {
    "7": 0.72,
    "9": 0.42,
    "6": 0.22
  },
  "bass_notes_per_2_bars": [
    5,
    9
  ],
  "swing_range": [
    0.14,
    0.3
  ],
  "optional_drum_density_budget": 7,
  "mutation_period_bars": [
    4,
    8,
    16
  ]
}
Generation recipe
- Choose a tempo inside 124-130 BPM and select a key from the weighted mode distribution.
 
- Select one progression template, transpose it, and generate three inversion candidates. Keep the lowest
 voice-leading-cost version.
- Generate a role-correct bass rhythm before decorative percussion. Reject collisions that weaken the kick.
 
- Create a short motif from scale degrees, then fit strong onsets to chord tones. Produce statement, answer, lift, and
 cadence variants.
- Apply the drum skeleton, optional-event budget, lane velocities, swing, and microtiming.
 
- Map the phrase into the arrangement state sequence. Every 4-8 bars, alter only one or two attributes.
 
- Run originality, range, density, collision, and phrase-coherence tests before export.
 
Failure modes
- Writing four large chords per bar and a busy lead above them.
 
- Randomizing every lane independently, destroying the shared pocket.
 
- Changing the bassline every bar instead of letting repetition work.
 
- Confusing minimal with empty: the remaining events must have strong placement and tone.
 
Originality mutations for this archetype
- Keep bass rhythm but rotate pitches through a new chord shell.
 
- Remove one percussion event and add a lower-velocity answer two steps later.
 
- Hold harmony static while moving only an inner voice.
 
- Delay the chord stab by one swung sixteenth and shorten its duration.

HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
22
PROFILE 3
KETTAMA
KETTAMA: High-impact loop pressure, hard drums,
and towering chord signals
Documented context
KETTAMA has publicly described an intuitive FL Studio process, a sample-heavy approach, and an appetite
for large chords, basslines, and hard drums. The concrete MIDI constraints below are generalized generator
rules, not transcriptions.
Generator identity
PARAMETER
DEFAULT / RANGE
Tempo
130-145 BPM; default 136
Core meter
4/4
Harmonic loop
1-4 bars; aggressive loop identity
Chord density
Low to medium; large gesture, simple content
Melody density
Low to medium; hooks from samples, stabs, or short riffs
Swing / microtiming
0.04-0.18; forward and forceful
Kick policy
Hard four-on-floor, hybrid, or rave/techno drive
Primary energy lever
Drum impact, sample repetition, chord scale, tension drops
Listening-based analysis
- Start with a loop that already feels forceful before adding arrangement. A strong kick, bass shape, and one hook can carry
 the identity.
- Harmonic sophistication matters less than scale, register, and rhythm. One huge chord stab can outperform a detailed
 progression.
- Use repetition to create pressure, then create contrast by muting the kick, filtering the loop, or exposing a vocal/sample
 fragment.
- At faster tempo, shorten notes and simplify syncopation enough to preserve impact.
 
Harmonic engine
ID
ROMAN NUMERALS
EXAMPLE IN KEY
GENERATOR USE
K1
i - bVII - bVI - bVII
E minor: Em - D - C - D
Rave loop with direct descending gravity
K2
i pedal + bII stab
F minor: Fm with occasional Gb/F
Dark tension; use bII as rare accent
K3
i - VI
C minor: Cm - Ab
Two-chord pressure; octave bass and huge top voice
K4
I - bVII
A major/mixolydian: A - G
Open, blunt, sample-friendly
K5
vi - IV - V
D major: Bm - G - A
Three-chord lift for a larger melodic section

- Use repetition to create pressure, then create contrast by muting the kick, filtering the loop, or exposing a vocal/sample
 fragment.
- At faster tempo, shorten notes and simplify syncopation enough to preserve impact.

HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
22
PROFILE 3
KETTAMA
KETTAMA: High-impact loop pressure, hard drums,
and towering chord signals
Documented context
KETTAMA has publicly described an intuitive FL Studio process, a sample-heavy approach, and an appetite
for large chords, basslines, and hard drums. The concrete MIDI constraints below are generalized generator
rules, not transcriptions.
Generator identity
PARAMETER
DEFAULT / RANGE
Tempo
130-145 BPM; default 136
Core meter
4/4
Harmonic loop
1-4 bars; aggressive loop identity
Chord density
Low to medium; large gesture, simple content
Melody density
Low to medium; hooks from samples, stabs, or short riffs
Swing / microtiming
0.04-0.18; forward and forceful
Kick policy
Hard four-on-floor, hybrid, or rave/techno drive
Primary energy lever
Drum impact, sample repetition, chord scale, tension drops
Listening-based analysis
- Start with a loop that already feels forceful before adding arrangement. A strong kick, bass shape, and one hook can carry
 the identity.
- Harmonic sophistication matters less than scale, register, and rhythm. One huge chord stab can outperform a detailed
 progression.
- Use repetition to create pressure, then create contrast by muting the kick, filtering the loop, or exposing a vocal/sample
 fragment.
- At faster tempo, shorten notes and simplify syncopation enough to preserve impact.

VOICING RULE
Use simple triads, fifth stacks, or sampled chord shapes with broad register. Double root or fifth in octaves and keep the
midrange uncluttered. Stabs can be 1-2 sixteenths at high velocity; breakdown chords may extend for a bar. A detuned
or sampled sound can provide complexity that the MIDI does not need to spell.
Melody engine
- Use 2-5 distinct pitches in a one- or two-bar hook.
 
- Favor minor pentatonic, natural minor, Dorian, or Mixolydian sets depending on mood.
 
- Repeat one onset pattern insistently; change register or last note after 2-4 bars.
 
- Allow octave displacement and repeated notes more than winding scalar lines.
 
- Keep pickups short at high BPM.
 
- If a sample is the hook, generate supporting MIDI that leaves its spectral and rhythmic space open.

HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
24
Parameter card
{
  "archetype": "high_impact_loop_pressure",
  "tempo": {
    "min": 130,
    "max": 145,
    "default": 136
  },
  "key_mode_weights": {
    "minor": 0.66,
    "major": 0.12,
    "dorian": 0.1,
    "mixolydian": 0.12
  },
  "hook_distinct_pitch_range": [
    2,
    5
  ],
  "chord_stab_duration_steps": [
    1,
    2
  ],
  "kick_anchor_probability": 1.0,
  "ride_entry_energy_threshold": 0.72,
  "tension_cut_bars": [
    4,
    8
  ]
}
Generation recipe
- Choose a tempo inside 130-145 BPM and select a key from the weighted mode distribution.
 
- Select one progression template, transpose it, and generate three inversion candidates. Keep the lowest
 voice-leading-cost version.
- Generate a role-correct bass rhythm before decorative percussion. Reject collisions that weaken the kick.
 
- Create a short motif from scale degrees, then fit strong onsets to chord tones. Produce statement, answer, lift, and
 cadence variants.
- Apply the drum skeleton, optional-event budget, lane velocities, swing, and microtiming.
 
- Map the phrase into the arrangement state sequence. Every 4-8 bars, alter only one or two attributes.
 
- Run originality, range, density, collision, and phrase-coherence tests before export.
 
Failure modes
- Adding complex jazz voicings where scale and impact should do the work.
 
- Programming weak velocities or long MIDI notes at fast tempo.
 
- Using nonstop fills so there is no pressure-release contrast.
 
- Mistaking loudness for composition; the loop still needs a distinct rhythmic identity.
 
Originality mutations for this archetype
- Keep the one-bar rhythm but reduce the hook pitch set from four notes to three.
 
- Move one root repetition up an octave at the phrase peak.
 
- Replace bVII motion with a pedal tone and rare bII tension hit.
 
- Create the second drop by subtraction before adding ride energy.

HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
25
PROFILE 4
AVICII
AVICII: Melody-first progressive house and
emotionally direct harmony
Documented context
Avicii publicly said he started with melody and would sit at the piano to play chords with the melody.
Image-Line's artist interview also describes melody as a durable core idea that was refined in harmony and
energy. The implementation details below are generalized analysis.
Generator identity
PARAMETER
DEFAULT / RANGE
Tempo
124-128 BPM; default 126
Core meter
4/4
Harmonic loop
4-8 bars; songlike harmonic sentences
Chord density
Medium; usually clear full progression
Melody density
Medium to high in hooks; singable contour
Swing / microtiming
0.00-0.10; generally straight and precise
Kick policy
Stable four-on-floor in dance sections
Primary energy lever
Chord emotion, memorable lead, octave layering, arrangement lift
Listening-based analysis
- The melody is the identity layer. Chords support its emotional reading and should remain clear enough for the hook to be
 singable.
- Use familiar diatonic functions with strong voice leading rather than complexity for its own sake.
 
- A lead can outline chord tones while using passing notes to create forward motion. Repetition is balanced by small tail
 changes and phrase peaks.
- The arrangement creates emotional scale by moving between intimate piano/guitar-like presentations and full dance
 layers.
Harmonic engine
ID
ROMAN NUMERALS
EXAMPLE IN KEY
GENERATOR USE
A1

HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
26
VOICING RULE
Begin with piano-friendly triads and inversions. Keep the top voice smooth and let the bass descend or rise purposefully.
Add sus2, add9, or octave doublings for openness, but do not obscure the chord function. In drops, layer the same MIDI
across complementary sounds rather than adding unrelated notes.
Melody engine
- Write a 1-2 bar motif that can be sung back after one hearing.
 
- Anchor important onsets to chord tones, especially thirds and roots.
 
- Use stepwise passing motion between chord targets.
 
- Create one clear register peak in bars 3-4 or 7-8.
 
- Repeat the motif with a changed ending; avoid constant new material.
 
- Use held notes or rests before a drop so the next attack feels larger.
 
- Test on plain piano. If the hook needs production to make sense, rewrite it.

Bass engine
Follow roots clearly, often on offbeat eighths or a sustained/pulsed pattern. Use inversions in the chord progression to
create a melodic bass line. Passing notes should connect roots, not compete with the lead.

HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
27
Parameter card
{
  "archetype": "melody_first_progressive_house",
  "tempo": {
    "min": 124,
    "max": 128,
    "default": 126
  },
  "key_mode_weights": {
    "major": 0.54,
    "minor": 0.46
  },
  "progression_length_bars": [
    4,
    8
  ],
  "lead_chord_tone_weight_on_strong_beats": 0.78,
  "lead_range_semitones": [
    9,
    16
  ],
  "phrase_peak_location": [
    0.58,
    0.82
  ],
  "swing_range": [
    0.0,
    0.1
  ]
}
Generation recipe
- Choose a tempo inside 124-128 BPM and select a key from the weighted mode distribution.
 
- Select one progression template, transpose it, and generate three inversion candidates. Keep the lowest
 voice-leading-cost version.
- Generate a role-correct bass rhythm before decorative percussion. Reject collisions that weaken the kick.
 
- Create a short motif from scale degrees, then fit strong onsets to chord tones. Produce statement, answer, lift, and
 cadence variants.
- Apply the drum skeleton, optional-event budget, lane velocities, swing, and microtiming.
 
- Map the phrase into the arrangement state sequence. Every 4-8 bars, alter only one or two attributes.
 
- Run originality, range, density, collision, and phrase-coherence tests before export.
 
Failure modes
- Starting from a sound-design trick without a durable melody and chord core.
 
- Using too many extensions or chromatic chords, clouding emotional directness.
 
- Writing a lead that never repeats or has no clear phrase peak.
 
- Copying the interval rhythm of a famous hook and merely transposing it.
 
Originality mutations for this archetype
- Retain the chord functions but design a new top-line rhythm before assigning pitches.
 
- Change the phrase peak from bar 4 to bar 3 and rewrite the cadence.
 
- Convert one repeated note into a stepwise passing pair.
 
- Use a new inversion bass line and a different opening scale degree.

- Run originality, range, density, collision, and phrase-coherence tests before export.
 
Failure modes
- Starting from a sound-design trick without a durable melody and chord core.
 
- Using too many extensions or chromatic chords, clouding emotional directness.
 
- Writing a lead that never repeats or has no clear phrase peak.
 
- Copying the interval rhythm of a famous hook and merely transposing it.
 
Originality mutations for this archetype
- Retain the chord functions but design a new top-line rhythm before assigning pitches.
 
- Change the phrase peak from bar 4 to bar 3 and rewrite the cadence.
 
- Convert one repeated note into a stepwise passing pair.
 
- Use a new inversion bass line and a different opening scale degree.

HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
28
PROFILE 5
DAVID GUETTA
DAVID GUETTA: Pop-EDM clarity, vocal space, and
scalable festival structure
Documented context
David Guetta has publicly emphasized that emotion comes from the music and chord progression. Sound
On Sound reporting on his collaborations describes choosing among chord progressions and then
developing the song and production. The rules below are broad pop-EDM analysis.
Generator identity
PARAMETER
DEFAULT / RANGE
Tempo
124-130 BPM for classic pop-EDM; preset can extend by era
Core meter
4/4
Harmonic loop
4-8 bars; chord progression supports topline
Chord density
Medium; clear harmonic skeleton
Melody density
Topline-led; instrumental hook complements vocal
Swing / microtiming
0.00-0.12; straight dance precision
Kick policy
Stable four-on-floor in dance sections
Primary energy lever
Songwriting core, vocal hook, build/drop contrast, broad voicings
Listening-based analysis
- The harmonic loop must support a strong vocal or lead topline. Clarity wins over ornamental harmony.
 
- Separate the vocal-hook register from the instrumental-hook register or alternate them in time.
 
- Build/drop structure scales a song core: remove bass and drums to expose emotion, then restore width, sub, and rhythmic
 drive.
- Collaboration-aware generation matters: produce slots for vocal, pre-chorus lift, post-chorus hook, and DJ-functional
 intros/outros.
Harmonic engine
ID
ROMAN NUMERALS
EXAMPLE IN KEY
GENERATOR USE
G1
vi - IV - I - V
C major: Am - F - C - G
Pop-EDM workhorse; strong for vocal chorus
G2
i - bVII - bVI
C minor: Cm - Bb - Ab
Direct minor drama; three-chord cycle
G3
I - V - IV - IV
D major: D - A - G - G
Open, simple, vocal-friendly
G4
IV - V - vi - vi
E major: A - B - C#m - C#m
Pre-chorus lift and delayed resolution
G5
i - VI - III - VII
F# minor: F#m - D - A - E
Festival minor-to-major openness
G6
I - iii - vi - IV
Bb major: Bb - Dm - Gm - Eb
Smooth pop movement and topline support

Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
29
VOICING RULE
Use piano or guitar-like triads to validate the song, then expand to octaves, fifth layers, and wide upper stacks. Keep the
midrange clear for vocals. Sus2 and add9 colors are safe when they do not conflict with the topline. Drop voicings can be
broad but should still share one harmonic skeleton.
Melody engine
- Reserve the strongest motif for either vocal chorus or post-chorus lead; do not make both equally busy.
 
- Use 3-6 note pitch vocabularies for highly memorable chorus cells.
 
- Repeat the title or hook rhythm with one lift note near the end.
 
- Aim for a clear two- or four-bar question-and-answer shape.
 
- Keep strong notes chord-aware and let passing notes connect them.
 
- For instrumental drops, simplify the chorus contour into a more rhythmic synth cell rather than duplicating every vocal
 note.
- Allow one pre-chorus ascent that raises pitch, density, or harmonic tension.

Bass engine
Use clear roots, fifths, and octave pulses. In vocal sections, simplify or sustain; in drops, use offbeat or eighth-note
propulsion. Follow slash-chord inversions when they improve the bass melody, but keep sub movement predictable
enough for festival translation.

8-16 bars

Final chorus
16-32 bars
Added harmony, octave, counterline, or extended outro

- Reserve the strongest motif for either vocal chorus or post-chorus lead; do not make both equally busy.
 
- Use 3-6 note pitch vocabularies for highly memorable chorus cells.
 
- Repeat the title or hook rhythm with one lift note near the end.
 
- Aim for a clear two- or four-bar question-and-answer shape.
 
- Keep strong notes chord-aware and let passing notes connect them.
 
- For instrumental drops, simplify the chorus contour into a more rhythmic synth cell rather than duplicating every vocal
 note.
- Allow one pre-chorus ascent that raises pitch, density, or harmonic tension.

Treat this grid as a skeleton. Primary hits are deterministic; open-dot positions are ghost or probability-controlled. Vary
velocity and microtiming within the profile's groove range.
Bass engine
Use clear roots, fifths, and octave pulses. In vocal sections, simplify or sustain; in drops, use offbeat or eighth-note
propulsion. Follow slash-chord inversions when they improve the bass melody, but keep sub movement predictable
enough for festival translation.
Arrangement state sequence
STATE
LENGTH
MIDI ACTION
Intro
8-16 bars
DJ-compatible rhythm or immediate song hook
Verse
8-16 bars
Reduced harmony and drums; vocal space
Pre-chorus
8 bars
Rising density, harmonic tension, snare or pulse
Chorus / drop
16 bars
Full bass, kick, wide harmony, hook
Verse 2 / break
8-16 bars
New supporting detail, reduced energy
Final chorus
16-32 bars
Added harmony, octave, counterline, or extended outro

v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
30
Parameter card
{
  "archetype": "pop_edm_clarity",
  "tempo": {
    "min": 124,
    "max": 130,
    "default": 128
  },
  "key_mode_weights": {
    "major": 0.48,
    "minor": 0.52
  },
  "vocal_space_priority": 0.88,
  "chorus_pitch_vocabulary_size": [
    3,
    6
  ],
  "drop_width_layer_count": [
    3,
    6
  ],
  "prechorus_rise_probability": 0.82,
  "swing_range": [
    0.0,
    0.12
  ]
}
Generation recipe
- Choose a tempo inside 124-130 BPM for classic pop-EDM and select a key from the weighted mode distribution.
 
- Select one progression template, transpose it, and generate three inversion candidates. Keep the lowest
 voice-leading-cost version.
- Generate a role-correct bass rhythm before decorative percussion. Reject collisions that weaken the kick.
 
- Create a short motif from scale degrees, then fit strong onsets to chord tones. Produce statement, answer, lift, and
 cadence variants.
- Apply the drum skeleton, optional-event budget, lane velocities, swing, and microtiming.
 
- Map the phrase into the arrangement state sequence. Every 4-8 bars, alter only one or two attributes.
 
- Run originality, range, density, collision, and phrase-coherence tests before export.
 
Failure modes
- Filling the midrange with chords and leads that leave no topline space.
 
- Treating the build as only a snare roll rather than harmonic and melodic expectation.
 
- Making the drop unrelated to the song's emotional core.
 
- Overcomplicating chord function when a simple loop would communicate more directly.
 
Originality mutations for this archetype
- Rewrite the chorus onset pattern while preserving only the phrase length.
 
- Move the instrumental hook into the gaps of the vocal rather than doubling it.
 
- Change the pre-chorus ascent into rhythmic compression instead of pitch ascent.
 
- Replace one chord with a held pedal and new bass inversion.

6. Generation order and dependency graph
Generation order matters because layers depend on one another. Tempo, meter, key, and archetype define the space.
Harmony defines stable pitch targets. Kick and harmonic rhythm shape bass. Bass and drums define groove. Melody
negotiates with harmony and vocal space. Arrangement decides when each layer appears. Humanization is applied after
the symbolic structure is accepted.
Do not generate all tracks independently and hope they align. Use shared objects: every note can query the current
chord, section energy, kick mask, density budget, and reserved register.
Recommended pipeline
STEP
MODULE
OUTPUT
HARD CHECK
1
Global setup
Tempo, key, scale, PPQ, archetype
Valid range and deterministic seed
2
Form
Section states and energy curve
Total length and transition logic
3
Harmony
Roman-numeral loop and voicings
Scale fit and voice-leading cost
4
Kick + backbeat
Anchor masks
Stable floor; no unintended gaps
5
Bass
Pitch and rhythm cell
Kick collision and chord fit
6
Chord rhythm
Stabs, sustains, or gates
Register and bass separation
7
Melody / hook
Motif plus phrase variants
Chord-tone, range, and originality checks
8
Hats + percussion
Groove detail
Density budget and timing coherence
9
Section mutation
Fills, mutes, lifts, reductions
Only controlled deltas per phrase
10
Humanization
Velocity, chance, microtiming
Anchor protection
11
Export
MIDI tracks plus metadata
Note bounds, ordering, no stuck notes
Shared context object

HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
33
7. Weighted constraints, not random choice
A useful generator combines hard constraints, weighted preferences, and controlled randomness. Hard constraints
prevent invalid or unmusical output: notes outside MIDI range, overlapping monophonic bass events, melody outside its
register, or too many optional drum hits. Soft scores decide among valid candidates.
Generate several candidates at each important stage, score them, and sample from the top group rather than always
choosing the single maximum. This preserves diversity without accepting obviously weak phrases.
Candidate score
score =
  1.6 * harmony_fit
+ 1.4 * groove_fit
+ 1.2 * motif_coherence
+ 1.0 * voice_leading
+ 0.9 * phrase_shape
+ 0.8 * archetype_fit
+ 0.6 * playable_range
+ 0.5 * novelty
- 1.8 * collision_penalty
- 2.2 * reference_similarity
- 1.0 * density_violation
Hard versus soft examples
RULE
TYPE
EXAMPLE
Scale membership
Soft
Chromatic approaches allowed with penalty or explicit rule
Strong-beat chord fit
Soft
Target 70-85%, not 100%
MIDI note 0-127
Hard
Reject out-of-range note
Bass monophony
Hard by default
No overlapping sub notes unless legato mode
Melody range
Hard within profile
Reject phrase beyond selected register
Optional drum budget
Hard
Do not exceed per-bar count
Motif repetition
Soft
Reward recognizable reuse but permit answer variation
Reference similarity
Hard threshold
Reject combined pitch-rhythm match
Top-k stochastic selection
After scoring, keep the top 10-25 percent of candidates and sample using a temperature parameter. Lower temperature
produces a more consistent preset; higher temperature explores more unusual but valid results. Never use temperature
to relax hard originality or MIDI-validity checks.

7. Weighted constraints, not random choice
A useful generator combines hard constraints, weighted preferences, and controlled randomness. Hard constraints
prevent invalid or unmusical output: notes outside MIDI range, overlapping monophonic bass events, melody outside its
register, or too many optional drum hits. Soft scores decide among valid candidates.
Generate several candidates at each important stage, score them, and sample from the top group rather than always
choosing the single maximum. This preserves diversity without accepting obviously weak phrases.
Candidate score
score =
  1.6 * harmony_fit
+ 1.4 * groove_fit
+ 1.2 * motif_coherence
+ 1.0 * voice_leading
+ 0.9 * phrase_shape
+ 0.8 * archetype_fit
+ 0.6 * playable_range
+ 0.5 * novelty
- 1.8 * collision_penalty
- 2.2 * reference_similarity
- 1.0 * density_violation
Hard versus soft examples
RULE
TYPE
EXAMPLE
Scale membership
Soft
Chromatic approaches allowed with penalty or explicit rule
Strong-beat chord fit
Soft
Target 70-85%, not 100%
MIDI note 0-127
Hard
Reject out-of-range note
Bass monophony
Hard by default
No overlapping sub notes unless legato mode
Melody range
Hard within profile
Reject phrase beyond selected register
Optional drum budget
Hard
Do not exceed per-bar count
Motif repetition
Soft
Reward recognizable reuse but permit answer variation
Reference similarity
Hard threshold
Reject combined pitch-rhythm match
Top-k stochastic selection
After scoring, keep the top 10-25 percent of candidates and sample using a temperature parameter. Lower temperature
produces a more consistent preset; higher temperature explores more unusual but valid results. Never use temperature
to relax hard originality or MIDI-validity checks.

34
8. Phrase, section, and arrangement state machines
A loop generator becomes a track generator only when it can create expectation and change. Define arrangement as a
state machine. Each state controls active layers, energy, register, note density, harmonic rhythm, and transition
probability. The archetype provides the most likely state order, but the exact bar counts can vary within allowed ranges.
Transitions should produce musical deltas. A lift may remove the kick, lengthen chords, raise melody register, and
reduce sub energy. A drop restores kick and bass, shortens chord articulation, and states the main motif. The model
should know which changes cause energy, rather than merely adding tracks.
Generic house state model
STATE
ENERGY
ACTIVE CORE
TRANSITION LEVER
INTRO
0.20-0.40
Kick or filtered drums, texture
Add bass or defining percussion
GROOVE_A
0.50-0.70
Kick, bass, core drums, restrained hook
Expose harmony or remove low end
LIFT
0.35-0.65
Harmony, vocal/lead hint, reduced floor
Riser, density ramp, held note
PEAK_A
0.75-0.95
Full groove and primary hook
Ride, octave, wider chord
BREAK
0.20-0.50
Song core or sample, minimal drums
Rebuild pulse and anticipation
PEAK_B
0.82-1.00
Full return plus one meaningful variation
Counterline, new tail, longer final phrase
OUTRO
0.20-0.55
DJ-functional drums, reduced hooks
Subtract bass and signature content
Energy control vector
{
  "energy": 0.84,
  "layer_count": 10,
  "kick_strength": 1.0,
  "sub_presence": 0.95,
  "top_end_density": 0.78,
  "chord_register": 0.72,
  "melody_register": 0.81,
  "note_density": 0.68,
  "stereo_width_hint": 0.82,
  "transition_fx_hint": 0.35
}
Variation budget
Within an established section, mutate one or two salient properties every 4-8 bars: remove a kick, add a pickup, change
the final bass note, open a hat, extend the chord tail, or answer the motif. At a major section boundary, change three or
more coordinated properties. This keeps micro-variation from competing with macro-form.

Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
35
9. Originality and similarity control
Artist-associated presets increase the risk that a generator will lean on a known record. Protect originality explicitly. The
best safeguard is not a disclaimer; it is a measurable rejection layer that compares symbolic fingerprints and requires
independent structural mutations.
Normalize melodies before comparison by converting absolute pitches to intervals, onsets to beat-relative gaps, and
durations to ratios. Compare short n-grams as well as whole phrases. A transposed copy will retain its interval and
rhythm fingerprints and should still be detected.
Similarity features
FEATURE
NORMALIZATION
USE
Pitch interval sequence
Subtract consecutive MIDI pitches
Detect transposed melodic copying
Rhythm interval sequence
Divide onset gaps by beat length
Detect same rhythm at new tempo
Duration ratio
Normalize to local subdivision
Detect articulation identity
Contour
Map intervals to up / same / down
Coarse shape similarity
Strong-beat degrees
Convert to scale degree over chord
Detect harmonic placement
Chord-function sequence
Roman numerals
Avoid entire progression-plus-rhythm identity
Drum onset masks
16-step binary lanes
Avoid copying signature drum breaks
Rejection policy
if melody_interval_similarity > 0.86
and melody_rhythm_similarity > 0.82:
    reject("hook too close")
if drum_mask_similarity > 0.90
and fill_location_match:
    mutate_drums()
if chord_sequence_match
and harmonic_rhythm_match
and bass_rhythm_similarity > 0.84:
    rewrite_two_independent_layers()
Minimum independent mutation set
- Change the onset pattern, not only the pitches.
 
- Change at least one phrase boundary or cadence behavior.
 
- Change the contour peak location.
 
- Change voicing and bass motion independently.
 
- Change drum or chord articulation if the reference identity depends on rhythm.
 
- Re-run similarity after mutation; do not assume changes are sufficient.

36
10. End-to-end pseudocode
function generate_track(request, seed):
    rng = seeded_random(seed)
    preset = load_archetype(request.archetype)
    global = choose_global_settings(request, preset, rng)
    form = build_arrangement_states(preset, request.length, rng)
    progression = sample_progression(preset, global.key, rng)
    voiced_chords = optimize_inversions(progression, preset.voicing)
    assert harmony_is_valid(voiced_chords, global.scale)
    kick = generate_kick(form, preset.kick_policy, rng)
    clap = generate_backbeat(form, preset, rng)
    bass_candidates = generate_bass(voiced_chords, kick, preset, rng, count=32)
    bass = select_top_k(bass_candidates, score_bass, temperature=0.35)
    chord_rhythm = articulate_chords(voiced_chords, form, preset, rng)
    motif_seed = generate_motif(progression, preset.melody, rng)
    phrase_candidates = develop_phrase(motif_seed, progression, preset, count=48)
    phrase = select_top_k(phrase_candidates, score_phrase, temperature=0.28)
    hats = generate_hats(kick, clap, preset.groove, rng)
    percussion = generate_optional_percussion(
        anchors=[kick, clap, hats],
        budget=preset.optional_drum_density_budget,
        rng=rng
    )
    tracks = map_layers_to_form(
        form, kick, clap, hats, percussion, bass, chord_rhythm, phrase
    )
    tracks = add_transition_events(tracks, form, preset, rng)
    tracks = apply_velocity_and_microtiming(tracks, preset, rng)
    assert midi_bounds_valid(tracks)
    assert density_valid(tracks, preset)
    assert groove_coherent(tracks, preset)
    assert reference_similarity(tracks) < request.similarity_threshold
    return export_midi(tracks, metadata={global, form, seed, preset.version})
Determinism and seeds
Every generation should save its seed, preset version, global settings, and mutation history. The same seed and code
version should recreate the same MIDI. This makes debugging possible and lets a user lock one layer while regenerating
another.
Layer locking
{
  "lock": [
    "tempo",
    "key",
    "chords",
    "kick"
  ],
  "regenerate": [
    "bass",
    "melody",
    "hats"
  ],
  "preserve_phrase_boundaries": true,
  "mutation_strength": 0.38
}

HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
37
11. Validation suite
Automated checks should be paired with listening, but they can eliminate many broken outputs. Run tests at event, bar,
phrase, section, and track levels. A generator brain becomes reliable when failures are named and measurable.
Event-level tests
- Pitch is an integer from 0 to 127.
 
- Velocity is an integer from 1 to 127 for note-on events.
 
- Duration is positive; paired note-off occurs after note-on.
 
- Events are sorted by performed time, then track priority.
 
- Monophonic bass notes do not overlap unless legato mode is enabled.
 
Bar-level tests
- Kick anchors match the selected policy.
 
- Optional drum hits do not exceed the density budget.
 
- Chord and bass pitches agree with the active harmony or an explicit approach rule.
 
- No register has excessive simultaneous note count.
 
- Microtiming does not reorder important anchors.
 
Phrase-level tests
- Motif appears in recognizable form at least twice.
 
- Statement and answer differ meaningfully but share an identity.
 
- Phrase has one main peak or deliberate flat/hypnotic profile.
 
- Cadence either closes or creates a clear pickup into the loop.
 
- Interval and rhythm fingerprints pass originality thresholds.
 
Section-level tests
- Energy direction matches the arrangement state.
 
- A transition changes at least the minimum number of coordinated features.
 
- The main hook is not active at full intensity in every section.
 
- Bass and kick return together only when intended.
 
- Fills occur near boundaries, not randomly throughout the section.
 
Listening checklist
SOLO / MUTE TEST
QUESTION
IF NO
Piano only
Do chords and melody communicate an idea?
Rewrite harmony or motif before sound design
Kick + bass
Does the low-end rhythm feel intentional?
Change onset relation and note lengths
Kick + clap + hat
Is the groove readable?
Simplify and repair hierarchy
No drums
Does the track still have phrase direction?
Improve harmonic/melodic form
No melody
Does the groove sustain interest?
Strengthen bass and micro-variation
Mono playback
Do essential roles remain clear?
Reduce register collisions and excess layering

Appendix A. Progression library
These are function templates, not artist transcriptions. Transpose freely, change harmonic rhythm, and use
archetype-specific voicing. The mood labels are tendencies rather than guarantees.

Transposition example
Template L02 is vi - IV - I - V. In C major it is Am - F - C - G. In E major it becomes C#m - A - E - B. The Roman numerals
and scale-degree melody stay the same; absolute MIDI pitches change. Recalculate inversions after transposition rather
than shifting every note blindly if the new register becomes too high or low.

v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
41
Appendix C. Archetype comparison matrix
ARCHETYPE
TEMPO
CHORDS
MELODY
GROOVE
MAIN ENERGY LEVER
PROSPA
124-134 BPM
Medium to high in peaks
Motif-led, medium
0.06-0.20
Piano/organ stabs, rave
motifs, breaks, octave lift
CHRIS STUSSY
124-130 BPM
Low
Low to medium
0.14-0.30
Bass rhythm, shuffle,
subtractive arrangement,
micro-variation
KETTAMA
130-145 BPM
Low to medium
Low to medium
0.04-0.18
Drum impact, sample
repetition, chord scale, tension
drops
AVICII
124-128 BPM
Medium
Medium to high in hooks
0.00-0.10
Chord emotion, memorable
lead, octave layering,
arrangement lift
DAVID GUETTA
124-130 BPM for classic
pop-EDM
Medium
Topline-led
0.00-0.12
Songwriting core, vocal hook,
build/drop contrast, broad
voicings
Fast preset selection
- Choose Prospa-associated rave-house when the request emphasizes piano/organ stabs, rave lift, and breakbeat
 memory.
- Choose Chris Stussy-associated minimal/deep tech when bass rhythm, swing, restraint, and long groove development
 are primary.
- Choose KETTAMA-associated high-impact pressure when the request asks for fast, hard, sample-led club force.
 
- Choose Avicii-associated melodic progressive house when a memorable instrumental melody and emotionally direct
 chords lead.
- Choose David Guetta-associated pop-EDM when a vocal-ready song core, build/drop clarity, and broad accessibility
 lead.
Hybridization policy
Hybrid presets should name one primary archetype and at most two borrowed dimensions. Example: primary Stussy
pocket, borrowed Prospa chord articulation, and Avicii phrase clarity. Do not average every parameter; that produces an
indistinct center. Preserve the primary preset's tempo, density, and arrangement, then borrow a specific melody,
harmony, or drum behavior.
{

Fast preset selection
- Choose Prospa-associated rave-house when the request emphasizes piano/organ stabs, rave lift, and breakbeat
 memory.
- Choose Chris Stussy-associated minimal/deep tech when bass rhythm, swing, restraint, and long groove development
 are primary.
- Choose KETTAMA-associated high-impact pressure when the request asks for fast, hard, sample-led club force.
 
- Choose Avicii-associated melodic progressive house when a memorable instrumental melody and emotionally direct
 chords lead.
- Choose David Guetta-associated pop-EDM when a vocal-ready song core, build/drop clarity, and broad accessibility
 lead.
Hybridization policy
Hybrid presets should name one primary archetype and at most two borrowed dimensions. Example: primary Stussy
pocket, borrowed Prospa chord articulation, and Avicii phrase clarity. Do not average every parameter; that produces an
indistinct center. Preserve the primary preset's tempo, density, and arrangement, then borrow a specific melody,
harmony, or drum behavior.

HOUSE MUSIC MIDI GENERATOR BRAIN
v1.0 - 23 JUL 2026
Original composition system - artist names used for analysis only
42
Appendix D. Prompt templates for a MIDI generator
Full-track prompt
Generate an original 128 BPM house MIDI arrangement in A minor.
Primary archetype: rave-house lift.
Use a new 4-bar progression selected from functional templates; do not imitate
any released hook. Chords should be short piano/organ stabs with smooth top-voice
movement. Create a 2-bar lead motif with 3-5 distinct pitches, one pickup, and one
octave accent. Use four-on-the-floor kick with a restrained breakbeat ghost layer.
Create a root-and-octave bass pattern that leaves kick space. Arrange 96 bars:
intro, pressure A, lift, peak A, switch, peak B, outro.
Return separate MIDI tracks plus JSON metadata and similarity-test results.
Regenerate only the melody
Keep tempo, key, chords, bass, drums, and section boundaries locked.
Generate 24 melody candidates for the selected archetype. Strong beats should
favor chord tones, total range must be 9-14 semitones, and the phrase needs one
peak between 60% and 80% of its duration. Select from the top 20% by motif
coherence and originality. Reject any candidate whose interval and rhythm
fingerprints both exceed the reference thresholds.
Generate a two-bar minimal groove
Create a 2-bar 127 BPM deep-tech MIDI groove in G minor.
Kick: stable four-on-floor. Clap: beats 2 and 4. Bass: 5-8 notes across two bars,
mostly root/fifth/octave plus at most one chromatic approach. Apply 0.18 swing to
eligible sixteenths. Add hats and percussion under an optional-hit budget of 7
per bar. Chord: one compact Gm9/Cm7 shell gesture with shared tones. Make bar 2
differ by only one bass event and one percussion event.
Machine-output contract
{
  "midi_files": [
    "kick.mid",
    "clap.mid",
    "hats.mid",
    "percussion.mid",
    "bass.mid",
    "chords.mid",
    "lead.mid"
  ],
  "metadata_required": [
    "seed",
    "tempo_bpm",
    "ppq",
    "key",
    "mode",
    "archetype",
    "progression_roman",
    "section_map",
    "similarity_scores"
  ],
  "validation_required": true
}

Chunking advice for retrieval systems
- Chunk by heading, not fixed character count, whenever possible.
 
- Keep an archetype's parameter card with its explanation.
 
- Store table captions and row text together.
 
- Attach metadata: archetype, layer, musical topic, evidence label, and page number.
 
- Retrieve one primary archetype section plus only the shared theory sections needed for the requested layer.
 
- Do not retrieve every artist profile for a single generation unless the request explicitly asks for a hybrid.

Appendix F. Glossary
TERM
DEFINITION
Anchor
A structurally reliable event such as a kick on each quarter note or clap on beats 2 and 4.
Archetype
A parameter preset capturing broad musical behaviors; not an artist clone.
Chord tone
A pitch belonging to the current chord, usually root, third, fifth, seventh, or chosen extension.
Contour
The up, down, and repeated-note shape of a melody independent of exact key.
Density budget
A maximum amount of note or onset activity allowed in a bar or layer group.
Ghost note
A low-velocity or probability-controlled event that supports groove without becoming an anchor.
Harmonic rhythm
The rate and placement at which chords change or articulate.
Inversion
A chord voicing with a chord tone other than the root in the lowest position.
Microtiming
Small performance offsets around the quantized grid.
Motif
A short, recognizable pitch-rhythm idea developed across a phrase.
Onset mask
A step-grid representation showing where events begin.
PPQ
Pulses per quarter note; MIDI time resolution.
Roman numeral
A key-relative label for chord function, enabling transposition.
Shell voicing
A reduced chord voicing, often third and seventh or root and seventh.
Strong beat
A metrically important location, especially beat 1 and other quarter-note anchors.
Swing
Systematic delay of selected subdivisions to create a long-short feel.
Top voice
The highest note in a chord voicing, often perceived melodically.
Voice leading
The movement of individual chord voices from one chord to the next.

References and source notes
Accessed 23 July 2026. These sources support documented workflow context and general MIDI implementation
concepts. All prescriptive parameter ranges, progression banks, pattern masks, and pseudocode in this document are
original synthesis and analysis.
#
SOURCE
RELEVANCE
URL
1
Avicii - Image-Line artist interview
Avicii discusses FL Studio, melody as an early core
idea, and refinement of harmony and energy.
https://www.image-line.com/artists/avicii-
2
Veni, Vidi, Avicii - Interview Magazine
Avicii states that he starts with the melody and sits at
the piano with chords and melody.
https://www.interviewmagazine.com/music/avicii-dj
3
In pictures: Avicii's Stockholm studio -
MusicRadar
Studio and workflow context, including Avicii's use of
FL Studio.
https://www.musicradar.com/news/tech/in-pictures-aviciis-st
ockholm-studio-508635
4
Artist Interview: Chris Stussy - Sample
Market
Public comments on studio discipline and
drum-machine use inside Fruity Loops / FL Studio.
https://samplemarket.co.uk/blog/artist-interview-chris-stussy
5
Chris Stussy: house music all night long - DJ
Mag
Career and production context, including continued FL
Studio use.
https://djmag.com/features/chris-stussy-house-music-all-nig
ht-long-interview
6
Introducing: KETTAMA - Beatportal
KETTAMA describes an intuitive, sample-heavy FL
Studio process and loop-led club music.
https://www.beatportal.com/articles/10950-introducing-ketta
ma
7
KETTAMA: The DJ Of The Moment -
Mixmag
Production background and discussion of large chords,
basslines, and forceful drums.
https://mixmag.net/feature/kettama-cover-feature-interview-
archangel-dj
8
David Guetta - Extended Interview -
Musician's Friend
Guetta emphasizes emotion, music, and chord
progression in his production process.
https://www.musiciansfriend.com/david-guetta-extended-int
erview
9
David Guetta - Sound On Sound
Collaboration and song-development context, including
choosing a chord progression.
https://www.soundonsound.com/people/david-guetta
10
Editing MIDI - Ableton Live 12 Manual
Official documentation for per-note MIDI editing,
velocity, probability, and related controls.
https://www.ableton.com/en/live-manual/12/editing-midi/
11

randomization, velocity, extraction, and committing
grooves.

scales, and note properties.
https://www.image-line.com/fl-studio-learning/fl-studio-onlin
e-manual/html/pianoroll.htm
Final implementation principle
Build a musician, not a slot machine
The generator should know why a note exists: harmony target, motif identity, groove role, energy transition,
or expressive variation. Randomness should choose among musically valid options. It should never be the
substitute for structure.

implied.
